title: Topic 169: The Psychology of Gaming Mentorship: Teaching Noobs
content:
# The Psychology of Gaming Mentorship: Teaching Noobs

## Abstract

This paper delves into the chaotic and often comical world of gaming mentorship, a realm where seasoned veterans take on the Sisyphean task of teaching “noobs” the arcane arts of digital dominance. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this curious relationship, from the altruistic desire to share knowledge to the narcissistic glee of watching a novice repeatedly walk into a wall. Drawing on theories of social learning, cognitive apprenticeship, and the ever-present Dunning-Kruger effect, we analyze the complex motivations of both mentor and mentee. Through a series of real-world examples and case studies from the annals of gaming history, we reveal how this seemingly trivial pursuit offers profound insights into human behavior, consumerism, and the eternal struggle between patience and the urge to scream, “No, the *other* left!”

## Introduction

In the grand tapestry of human achievement, few endeavors are as noble, as fraught with peril, or as utterly absurd as teaching a new player—a “noob”—how to play a video game. It is a journey into the heart of darkness, a test of patience that would make a saint swear, and a psychological drama played out in pixels and polygons. This paper, therefore, ventures into the unexplored territory of gaming mentorship, a field of study that has for too long been relegated to the footnotes of academic inquiry. We seek to understand the strange and wonderful bond that forms between the seasoned veteran and the fumbling novice, a relationship that is part teacher-student, part drill sergeant-recruit, and part exasperated parent-toddler who has just discovered the joy of sticking forks in electrical sockets. Why do we do it? What compels a player who has spent thousands of hours mastering a game to turn around and offer a helping hand to someone who can’t figure out how to jump? Is it altruism? A thirst for validation? Or is it a form of digital schadenfreude, a secret delight in watching others fail where we have succeeded? This paper will argue that it is all of these things and more. We will explore the psychological motivations, the communication breakdowns, and the surprising moments of triumph that define the mentorship experience in online gaming. From the heroic Sherpas of *Destiny* to the long-suffering squad leaders of *Call of Duty*, we will examine the unsung heroes of the gaming world, the patient souls who guide us through our first raids, our first headshots, and our first of many, many, *many* deaths. So, grab your headset, chug a suspiciously-colored energy drink, and prepare to dive into the hilarious, frustrating, and ultimately rewarding world of teaching noobs.

## Literature Review

The hallowed halls of academia have, for the most part, remained blissfully ignorant of the pedagogical pandemonium that is gaming mentorship. However, by cobbling together disparate threads of research from psychology, education, and media studies, we can begin to construct a theoretical framework for understanding this phenomenon. At the forefront of our inquiry is Albert Bandura’s Social Learning Theory, which posits that people learn from one another via observation, imitation, and modeling. In the context of gaming, this theory is on full display as the noob watches the mentor’s every move, attempting to replicate their god-like reflexes and strategic genius. Of course, this process is often less “monkey see, monkey do” and more “monkey see, monkey fall off a cliff.” The literature on Cognitive Apprenticeship, which outlines how masters of a craft teach their skills to novices, also provides a useful lens through which to view gaming mentorship. This model emphasizes the importance of scaffolding, coaching, and fading, as the mentor gradually withdraws support as the mentee’s skills develop. In the gaming world, this translates to the mentor initially holding the noob’s hand (metaphorically, of course, unless you’re into that kind of thing) and then slowly letting go, hoping they don’t immediately run into a boss battle with a wooden sword. Finally, no discussion of gaming and skill would be complete without mentioning the Dunning-Kruger effect, the cognitive bias wherein people with low ability at a task overestimate their ability. This effect is rampant in the gaming community, with noobs who have just learned how to walk and chew bubblegum simultaneously declaring themselves ready to take on the world champion. The mentor, in this scenario, becomes a kind of reality-check, a gentle (or not-so-gentle) reminder that there is a vast chasm between perceived skill and actual competence. The interplay of these psychological principles creates a rich and complex dynamic, a dance of ego, empathy, and the occasional rage-quit that is as fascinating as it is funny.

## Analysis

To truly comprehend the intricate ballet of bullets and blunders that is gaming mentorship, we must dissect the psychological drivers of its participants. The mentor, a figure of supposed wisdom and digital prowess, is not merely a benevolent guide. Their motivations are a complex cocktail of altruism, ego, and a dash of sadism. On one hand, there is the genuine, almost paternal, desire to foster a new generation of players. This is the mentor as the community’s gatekeeper and cheerleader, driven by a vision of a thriving, non-toxic player base. They are the Gandalf to the noob’s Frodo, guiding them through the treacherous mines of multiplayer lobbies. However, let us not be naive. The ego plays a significant role. The mentor, often a player who has reached the endgame and has nothing left to prove, finds a new stage for their talents. Teaching a noob is a performance, a live demonstration of their mastery. Every successful instruction, every “wow, you’re so good!” from the mentee, is a hit of dopamine, a validation of the thousands of hours they’ve poured into the game. This is consumer loyalty in its most potent form; the mentor becomes an unpaid brand ambassador, selling the game experience to the new consumer (the noob) with a passion that no marketing campaign can replicate.

On the other side of the headset is the noob, a creature of pure, unadulterated incompetence. Their psychological state is a whirlwind of confusion, frustration, and the occasional spark of brilliance. The most fascinating specimen is the Dunning-Kruger noob, a player whose confidence is inversely proportional to their skill. They have just learned to reload their weapon, and they are already critiquing the mentor’s loadout. “I saw a streamer do it this way,” they proclaim, moments before walking off a cliff. This is where the mentor’s patience is truly tested. The mentor must navigate the treacherous waters of the noob’s ego, offering advice without triggering their defensive mechanisms. The mentor acts as a gentle (or not-so-gentle) corrective to the noob's inflated self-perception, a process that is as painful as it is necessary for growth. Then there is the eager apprentice, the rare and beautiful creature who hangs on the mentor’s every word. They are a sponge, soaking up knowledge and executing instructions with a heartening, if clumsy, enthusiasm. For the mentor, finding an eager apprentice is like finding a unicorn in a loot box; it is a magical experience that restores their faith in the gaming community. The initial experience of the noob, heavily influenced by the mentor, is a critical factor in player retention. A positive mentorship experience can turn a fumbling first-time player into a loyal, long-term consumer. A negative one can send them running for the hills, or worse, to a rival game.

The dynamic between mentor and mentee is a psychological tightrope walk. The “curse of knowledge” is a constant obstacle, with the mentor forgetting the sheer terror and confusion of being a beginner. “Just kite the adds and DPS the boss,” the mentor says, as the noob, who thinks “kiting” is something you do in a park, is promptly devoured by a swarm of low-level enemies. The communication is a comedy of errors, a mix of jargon, insults, and the occasional, beautiful moment of clarity. This is the crucible of mentorship, where patience is forged and friendships are born. The ultimate reward, the psychological payoff that keeps the cycle going, is the “aha!” moment. It is the moment the noob finally lands a headshot, executes a perfect combo, or simply manages to not die for a full minute. In that moment, the mentor’s frustration melts away, replaced by a profound sense of pride. They have passed on the torch, they have made a difference, and they have ensured that the grand, chaotic tradition of gaming will continue for another generation.

## Real-World Examples

The annals of gaming are replete with tales of mentorship, both triumphant and tragic. One of the most celebrated examples is the “Adopt-a-Noob” program in the sprawling space opera *EVE Online*. In a game notorious for its brutal learning curve and cutthroat player base, veteran players volunteer to take new players under their wing, teaching them the intricacies of spaceship piloting, market trading, and corporate espionage. This is not just about teaching game mechanics; it is about cultural indoctrination. The mentor is not just a teacher; they are a recruiter, a protector, and a guide to the complex social and political landscape of the game. The success of this program is a testament to the power of structured mentorship in fostering player retention and community growth. It is a masterclass in turning a bewildering and intimidating experience into a compelling and addictive one.

On the other end of the spectrum, we have the countless, undocumented instances of informal mentorship that occur every day in the trenches of online gaming. Consider the archetypal *Call of Duty* lobby, a digital petri dish of human nature. Here, mentorship is often a baptism by fire. A grizzled veteran, weary of watching his team get repeatedly wiped out, will take a struggling noob aside and offer some gruff, expletive-laden advice. “For the love of all that is holy, stop running into the open like a headless chicken!” he might scream, before demonstrating the ancient and sacred art of “camping” in a corner. This is not the gentle, nurturing mentorship of *EVE Online*. This is tough love, a brutal but effective method of instruction. The noob, if they can withstand the verbal assault, will emerge from the experience a hardened and more competent player. This is the consumer being forged in the fires of competition, their loyalty to the game (and their mentor) solidified through shared adversity.

Then there are the truly bizarre and hilarious examples of mentorship gone wrong. The internet is littered with stories of well-intentioned mentors who have been driven to the brink of madness by their clueless charges. There is the tale of the *World of Warcraft* player who spent three hours trying to teach his friend how to fish, only to discover that his friend had been trying to cast his line into a puddle. There is the *League of Legends* mentor who, after a particularly grueling session with a noob who insisted on building a character with completely inappropriate items, simply logged off and uninstalled the game. These stories, while comical, highlight the immense emotional labor involved in gaming mentorship. They are a reminder that for every successful mentorship, there are a dozen that end in frustration, rage, and the quiet, dignified sound of a keyboard being thrown against a wall. These are the cautionary tales that circulate within the gaming community, the folklore of the digital age, warning of the perils of taking on a noob with an ego bigger than their skill bar.

## Discussion

The seemingly frivolous act of teaching a noob how to play a video game has surprisingly profound implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. In the microcosm of the gaming world, we see the fundamental principles of social interaction, learning, and community formation play out in accelerated and often exaggerated form. The mentor-noob relationship is a case study in the power of social influence and the delicate dance of ego and empathy. It demonstrates that even in the anonymous and often hostile environment of online gaming, the human desire to connect, to teach, and to learn can triumph over toxicity.

From a consumer behavior perspective, gaming mentorship is a powerful and often overlooked driver of player engagement and retention. A positive mentorship experience can be the deciding factor that turns a casual player into a devoted fan and a paying customer. The mentor, in this context, is the ultimate brand ambassador, a trusted and authentic voice that can evangelize a game far more effectively than any marketing campaign. Game developers and publishers who understand and leverage this dynamic, by creating and supporting mentorship programs, are investing in the long-term health and profitability of their communities. The noob who is successfully mentored is not just a retained customer; they are a future mentor, a perpetuator of the cycle, and a valuable asset to the game’s ecosystem.

Furthermore, the study of gaming mentorship offers a unique window into the future of education and training. The principles of cognitive apprenticeship, once confined to the traditional master-apprentice relationship, are now being applied in the digital realm with remarkable success. The use of scaffolding, modeling, and fading, all staples of gaming mentorship, has the potential to revolutionize how we teach and learn in a variety of fields. The virtual worlds of online games are not just playgrounds; they are powerful learning environments, where complex skills can be taught and mastered in a safe and engaging way. The lessons we learn from the often-chaotic world of gaming mentorship may one day inform the design of our schools, our workplaces, and our society as a whole.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the act of teaching a noob is far more than a simple transfer of information. It is a complex psychological and social phenomenon, a microcosm of the human condition played out in a digital arena. It is a story of patience and frustration, of ego and empathy, of failure and triumph. The relationship between the mentor and the noob, in all its chaotic glory, is a testament to the enduring power of human connection in an increasingly virtual world. It is a reminder that even in a world of pixels and polygons, the most important game we play is the one we play together. So, the next time you see a seasoned veteran patiently guiding a clueless noob through a treacherous digital landscape, take a moment to appreciate the beautiful, frustrating, and profoundly human drama that is unfolding before your eyes. And if you are that veteran, take a deep breath, count to ten, and for the love of all that is holy, don’t let them touch the shiny red button.
references:
Bandura, A. (1977). *Social learning theory*. Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice Hall.

Collins, A., Brown, J. S., & Newman, S. E. (1989). Cognitive apprenticeship: Teaching the crafts of reading, writing, and mathematics. In L. B. Resnick (Ed.), *Knowing, learning, and instruction: Essays in honor of Robert Glaser* (pp. 453-494). Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

Kruger, J., & Dunning, D. (1999). Unskilled and unaware of it: How difficulties in recognizing one's own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 77*(6), 1121–1134.

Kowert, R., & Oldmeadow, J. A. (2014). The relationship between online video game involvement and gaming-related friendships among emotionally sensitive individuals. *Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking, 17*(7), 447-453.

Steinkuehler, C., & Williams, D. (2006). Where everybody knows your (screen) name: Online games as “third places.” *Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication, 11*(4), 885-909.
